All Questions
Were You Asked This?
Design a global VM monitoring system

Design a system that allows users to view and monitor their virtual machines across multiple global regions, displaying key information such as node name, region, and health status.

Asked at:

Google

A global VM monitoring system is a control-plane dashboard and backend that aggregates health signals from virtual machines running in many regions, then lets users see a unified list with node name, region, and status, much like AWS CloudWatch, Azure Monitor, or Datadog’s infrastructure list. Users open a single console, filter by region or tag, and get near real-time status, alerts, and drill-down details.

Interviewers ask this to assess if you can design a multi-region, event-driven system that ingests high-volume heartbeats, computes health correctly, and serves low-latency, real-time views. They’re looking for clarity on consistency vs freshness, fan-in/fan-out paths, caching strategies, failure isolation, and how you keep costs and complexity under control while meeting tight UX expectations.

Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to view a unified list of their VMs across regions with node name, region, and current health status.

Users should be able to filter, sort, and search VMs by attributes such as region, status, tags, and node name.

Users should be able to receive near real-time updates as VM health changes without manual refresh.

Users should be able to configure and receive alerts on health changes and view recent status history for a VM.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How do you ingest and compute health from high-volume, multi-region heartbeats while keeping the latest status accurate and timely?

How do you push near real-time updates to thousands of dashboards without overwhelming your backend?

How do you design for multi-region resiliency and data locality while providing a global view?

How do you keep read latency low for list pages with rich filters (status, region, tags) as the fleet grows?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Real-time Updates

Users expect the dashboard to reflect VM health changes within seconds. Applying the real-time updates pattern clarifies push vs pull, WebSockets/SSE choices, fanout, and how to throttle or batch updates to many clients efficiently.

Read More
Scaling Writes

Heartbeats and status signals are a high-volume, bursty write stream. You need a durable buffer, idempotent processors, and storage tuned for frequent upserts of the latest state without hotspots.

Read More
Scaling Reads

The main UI is read-heavy with low-latency expectations and flexible filters. Caches, read-optimized indexes, and precomputed aggregates keep list views snappy at scale.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Kafka

Kafka is a strong fit for buffering and ordering heartbeat events, enabling regional ingestion, replay for backfills, and fanout to consumers that compute latest health, trigger alerts, and update caches.

Read More
Redis

Redis provides a low-latency cache for the latest VM state and aggregate counters, supports TTLs for heartbeat expiry, and can drive pub/sub channels for pushing incremental updates to the web tier.

Read More
DynamoDB

DynamoDB Global Tables offer multi-region active-active storage for the authoritative latest VM state with conditional writes; secondary indexes help list/filter by tenant, region, and status while keeping operations operationally simple.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

FB Live Comments

Both require near real-time fanout of frequent updates to many clients, with considerations for WebSockets/SSE, batching, and filtering by subscription context (tenant/stream).

Read
Practice
Uber

Driver location updates resemble VM heartbeats: high-frequency writes, last-write-wins semantics, and a read path that needs fast, fresh state with spatial/attribute filters.

Read
Practice
Ad Click Aggregator

High-velocity event ingestion, deduplication, and aggregate computation mirror monitoring pipelines that must process and summarize signals reliably at scale.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Relying solely on frequent client polling for freshness

Aggressive polling from thousands of dashboards will overload read paths, increase costs, and still deliver poor latency under load. Interviewers expect a push-based or hybrid approach with batching and throttling.

No clear health semantics or TTLs for heartbeats

If you don’t define expiry windows, grace periods, and last-write-wins rules, the system will flap statuses or show stale data, eroding trust and making alerting noisy and unreliable.

Single-region control plane without failure isolation

Centralizing ingestion and state in one region creates a global blast radius. A regionalized ingest/compute layer with replicated latest state is expected for resiliency and low latency.
